events.title: WTI crude oil surges back above $100/barrel on Middle East conflict escalation
events.summary: Ceasefire hopes faded during the week ending March 27, 2026, pushing WTI crude back above $100/barrel. The ongoing war in Iran is the dominant macro theme, driving energy prices higher and creating stagflationary pressures across markets.
events.why_now: Ceasefire negotiations broke down during the week, reversing an earlier oil price decline. The $100 threshold is psychologically significant and triggers reassessment of inflation forecasts, corporate margins, and consumer spending power.
events.market_relevance: Oil above $100 raises input costs across transportation, manufacturing, chemicals, and agriculture. It compresses consumer discretionary spending, boosts energy sector revenues, and forces the Fed to weigh inflation risk against growth slowdown. This is the single most important macro variable for cross-asset allocation right now.

events.title: 10-year Treasury yield rises to 4.43%, above Nuveen's forecast range ceiling
events.summary: The 10-year Treasury yield rose 5 bps to 4.43% for the week, now sitting above Nuveen's projected 4.00%-4.25% year-end range. The move was driven by oil-fueled inflation repricing. Month-to-date, the 10-year yield has risen 49 bps.
events.why_now: The combination of persistent core inflation (PCE at 3.1%) and a new energy price shock is forcing the long end of the curve higher. A 49 bps monthly move in the 10-year is a significant repricing event that affects discount rates across all asset classes.
events.market_relevance: Higher long-end yields directly increase mortgage rates, reduce equity valuations (especially growth/tech), raise corporate borrowing costs, and pressure leveraged balance sheets. The speed of the move matters as much as the level—rapid repricing can trigger margin calls and portfolio rebalancing.

events.title: Credit spreads widen broadly; high yield underperforms by 46 bps in one week
events.summary: High yield corporate bonds underperformed duration-equivalent Treasuries by 46 bps, preferred securities by 45 bps, and emerging markets by 23 bps for the week. CMBS and MBS were relative outperformers with minimal spread widening.
events.why_now: The spread widening reflects growing concern about a stagflationary scenario: higher oil prices simultaneously raise inflation and threaten corporate earnings and economic growth. This is a classic risk-off signal in credit markets.
events.market_relevance: Widening HY spreads signal tightening financial conditions for lower-rated borrowers. Companies reliant on high-yield debt markets for refinancing face higher costs. The divergence between CMBS (relatively stable) and HY corporates suggests the market sees energy-sensitive corporate sectors as more vulnerable than real estate credit at this stage.

events.title: Fed rate cut expectations collapse to one cut in 2026; market aligns with hawkish dot plot
events.summary: Markets have shifted from pricing over two rate cuts for 2026 to aligning with the FOMC's view of at most one cut. Nuveen still expects two cuts but acknowledges the second may be pushed into 2027. The oil shock has effectively eliminated near-term easing expectations.
events.why_now: The March FOMC meeting (held March 18) combined with the oil price surge has forced a dramatic repricing. Core PCE at 3.1% and rising energy costs make it nearly impossible for the Fed to cut in the near term despite a weakening labor market.
events.market_relevance: Fewer expected rate cuts mean higher-for-longer short-term rates, which keeps money market yields attractive (competing with equities for flows), maintains pressure on floating-rate borrowers, and constrains housing market recovery. The Fed is caught between conflicting mandates.

events.title: February NFP showed unexpected loss of 92,000 jobs; unemployment rate rises to 4.4%
events.summary: The February non-farm payrolls report showed the US economy unexpectedly lost 92,000 jobs versus an expected gain of 55,000. The unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4%, continuing a slow but steady rise from the 3.4% low in April 2023.
events.why_now: This data, released before the time window but still being digested by markets, represents a significant deterioration in labor market conditions. Combined with the oil shock, it raises the probability of a stagflationary outcome where the Fed cannot easily respond to either problem.
events.market_relevance: A negative payrolls print of this magnitude is rare outside recessions. It undermines the 'strong US growth story' narrative and raises questions about consumer spending sustainability. The March jobs report (due end of April) becomes a critical confirmation or refutation point.

events.title: Fed Chair Powell succession uncertainty: DOJ investigation and Warsh nomination stalled
events.summary: Fed Chair Powell is pledging to stay until a DOJ investigation concludes or is dismissed. Senator Thom Tillis is blocking the nomination of Kevin Warsh as Powell's successor. Warsh has historically been hawkish but recently shifted dovish in alignment with the President's preferences.
events.why_now: The leadership uncertainty at the Fed adds a layer of policy unpredictability at a moment when markets need clarity on the reaction function. Whether the next Chair is dovish or hawkish materially affects forward rate expectations and asset pricing.
events.market_relevance: Fed leadership transitions historically create volatility in rates markets. If Warsh eventually takes over with a dovish mandate from the President, it could lead to earlier rate cuts than currently priced—but his hawkish history creates ambiguity. The DOJ investigation itself raises institutional credibility concerns for the Fed.

events.title: Core PCE inflation at 3.1% and rising, with tariff pass-through effects still incomplete
events.summary: Core PCE rose for a second consecutive month to 3.1% in January 2026, well above the Fed's 2% target. Sources note that the full effect of tariffs has not yet fed through to prices, meaning inflation may have structural upward pressure even without the Iran war's energy price impact.
events.why_now: The layering of multiple inflationary forces—persistent services inflation, incomplete tariff pass-through, and now an energy shock—creates a compounding inflation problem that is qualitatively different from any single driver.
events.market_relevance: Persistent above-target inflation erodes real returns on fixed income, pressures the Fed to maintain restrictive policy, and compresses real wage growth (undermining consumer spending). Companies with pricing power outperform; those that cannot pass through costs see margin compression.

events.title: Municipal bonds suffer sharp losses: -2.72% month-to-date, worst among fixed income sectors
events.summary: The municipal bond index returned -0.81% for the week and -2.72% month-to-date, the worst performance among all fixed income sectors tracked by Nuveen. Taxable municipals were also down -3.08% month-to-date. High yield munis held up slightly better at -2.38% MTD.
events.why_now: Rising Treasury yields hit long-duration munis particularly hard (effective duration 6.81 years). The combination of rate moves and potential tax-loss selling or fund outflows may be creating a negative feedback loop in muni markets.
events.market_relevance: Municipal bond weakness directly affects state and local government borrowing costs, potentially constraining infrastructure spending and public services. For investors, the sharp drawdown may trigger outflows from muni funds, creating further selling pressure. However, the dislocation may also create buying opportunities for tax-sensitive investors.
